track: 3 â€” recall/discovery (Shape-B precision-silence on a new stack: Go) + 1 LOW real coupling
shape: B-with-one-LOW (precision-silence across 5 cross-module families + a single LOW IF-3a real coupling, recorded NOT filed â€” the value is the named-suppression precision story on a mature Go codebase)
engine_proxy: deterministic-detector-sweep (5 read-only agents â€” IF-8 / IF-6 / IF-10 / IF-7c / IF-3a â€” applying the SHIPPED deterministic predicates (Â§21/Â§24+Â§28/Â§25+Â§29/Â§23/AF-1) directly to the real Go source; NOT a full Detectâ†’Emit pipeline; no cost ledger recorded â€” cost is the Track-2 ledgers. Build chain:  (6 agents, ~318k tokens, 103 tool-uses).

repo.name: caddyserver/caddy
repo.pinned_sha_note: shallow-clone HEAD at 2026-06-12; the file:line citations are the durable anchors.
repo.stack: Go (web server / reverse proxy) â€” the FIRST Go-stack datapoint in the discovery track (prior sweeps: Crystal/kemal, Python/pyccel, TypeScript/excalidraw)
repo.size: 318 Go files, 71,557 Go code LOC (scc), M-tier; ~6 component buckets (caddyconfig, cmd, internal, modules, notify, root) over 46 real non-test packages / 195 import edges

analysis_scope: whole-repo (all 318 Go files; IF-8 built the real 46-package / 195-edge import graph from non-test files)
result_headline: Across five families on caddy@fcba554d (~318 Go files / ~71.5k LOC), the guarded sweep produced exactly 1 fire (a single LOW IF-3a â€” cmd re-assigns the root package's two exported mutable globals caddy.DefaultStorage / caddy.ConfigAutosavePath cross-component with no setter, duplicating storage.go's derivation) against 38 named suppressions (IF-8 4 / IF-6 4 / IF-10 6 / IF-7c 15 / IF-3a 9), a precision-first verdict in which every non-fire is mechanism-named as predicate-FALSE rather than missed â€” the expected mature-codebase outcome with honest scope at the language-enforced level.

suppressed.reason: NAMED SUPPRESSION (coarse-bucket-artifact, not a true import cycle). The component-bucket graph shows a mutual caddyconfig<->modules pair (caddyconfig/httpcaddyfile imports modules/caddyhttp here; conversely modules/caddyevents/eventsconfig/caddyfile.go:24 imports caddyconfig). But these are DISJOINT packages on each side: caddyconfig/httpcaddyfile is NOT imported back by modules/caddyhttp (verified: grep of modules/caddyhttp/*.go for caddyconfig/httpcaddyfile = empty). The IF-8 predicate requires a TRUE import cycle (a real cyclic dependency / SCC>=2 on the actual import unit), and the real Go-package SCC analysis (46 pkgs, 195 non-test edges) yields ZERO cycles. Firing on the collapsed top-level bucket would be the exact naive over-fire the predicate's 'structural import cycle (compile-time)' wording excludes.

NAMED SUPPRESSION (coarse-bucket-artifact). Half of the mutual cmd<->modules bucket pair: modules/caddyhttp imports the root cmd package here, while conversely cmd/caddy/main.go:37 (package main) imports modules/standard. Disjoint packages: cmd/caddy is a leaf 'package main' that NOTHING imports (verified: grep for caddyserver/caddy/v2/cmd/caddy = empty), so it can never be a cycle node. No package transitively imports back to its importer. Not a true import cycle; real Go-package graph is a proven DAG.

NAMED SUPPRESSION (test-helper edge + coarse-bucket-artifact). This is the ONLY internal->modules edge (internal/testmocks imports modules/caddytls) and the lone internal->root edge (dummyverifier.go:6 imports root). testmocks is a test-mock helper package; the reverse modules->internal / root->internal edges go to the real internal/* utility packages (e.g. modules/caddyhttp/autohttps.go:28, admin.go:49). Disjoint packages on each side; internal/* is a leaf utility tree imported BY many but its testmocks subpkg importing up does not close a real cycle. Real Go-package SCC = 0.

NAMED SUPPRESSION (coarse-bucket-artifact, reverse direction of the dominant modules<->caddyconfig pair, 103 modules->caddyconfig vs 18 caddyconfig->modules edges). modules/*/caddyfile.go subpackages import caddyconfig (the Caddyfile-adapter surface) while caddyconfig/httpcaddyfile imports the modules/caddyhttp parent package. This is the canonical layered pattern (config adapters depend on module types; module subpackages register Caddyfile directives) â€” disjoint package sets, no same-package back-import. The real Go-package import graph is acyclic (Go compile-time cycle ban; caddy builds), so the predicate's true-cycle requirement is FALSE.

per_family.go_specific_note: Go FORBIDS package import cycles at COMPILE time: the compiler rejects any cyclic import among packages, so a buildable Go module CANNOT contain a true package-level import cycle. Verified directly: I built the real Go-package import graph (import path = directory) over non-test files = 46 packages / 195 directed edges and ran Tarjan SCC -> ZERO strongly-connected components of size>=2 (proven DAG). This is the unit IF-8's 'structural import cycle (compile-time)' predicate actually targets, and it is acyclic. SEPARATELY, the coarse component-bucket graph (collapsing all packages into the 6 named top-level groups caddyconfig/cmd/internal/modules/notify/root) DOES produce a size-5 SCC {caddyconfig,cmd,internal,modules,root} with 4 mutual pairs (caddyconfig<->modules, cmd<->modules, internal<->modules, internal<->root). A NAIVE component-level detector would fire 1-4 IF-8 issues here. The precision-first / guarded predicate does NOT fire, because every mutual bucket pair is composed of DISJOINT packages on each side (e.g. caddyconfig/httpcaddyfile->modules/caddyhttp but modules/caddyhttp/encode->caddyconfig/httpcaddyfile; cmd/caddy[main]->modules/standard but modules/caddyhttp->cmd) â€” never a package importing a package that transitively imports it back. The bucket SCC is a pure granularity artifact of mapping a clean layered package DAG onto 6 oversized component buckets, not a real cyclic dependency. STRUCTURAL FACT RECORDED: at the language-enforced level caddy has 0 import cycles; the apparent cycle exists ONLY at the lossy component-grouping abstraction and is correctly suppressed. (Scope note: caddytest/ is a 7th top-level dir not in the 5 named components; it was classified separately and only ever appears as a source of edges (test harness), never creating a back-edge into a cycle.) Net: 0 genuine fires, 4 named suppressions â€” the expected mature-codebase outcome for a deterministic structural family against a language with a hard compile-time cycle ban.

suppressed.reason: MatcherNameCtxKey duplicated across 2 components (caddyconfig/caddyfile/dispenser.go:533 AND modules/caddyhttp/celmatcher.go:833) â€” the ONLY cross-component same-name collision in the tree. Suppressed on TWO independent predicate-FALSE grounds: (1) SAME-VALUE â€” both are the identical scalar literal "matcher_name", so the symmetric difference is empty; IF-6 requires a non-empty symmetric difference and must NEVER fire on agreement (it is the polarity-opposite of a clone detector). (2) NON-ENUMERABLE / not-a-set â€” these are single-valued scalar string consts, not named value-SETS; the IF-6 deterministic slice (Â§24/Â§28) covers []string / enum / z.enum / frozenset, and the spec explicitly leaves divergent named SCALARS to the deferred semantic layer (a scalar entails nothing). This is a deliberate shared context-key convention; firing would be a textbook FP.

defaultProtocols=["h1","h2","h3"] (caddyhttp) vs allowedVersions=["1.1","2","h2c","3"] (caddyhttp/reverseproxy, httptransport.go:178) vs defaultALPN=["h2","http/1.1"] (caddytls, connpolicy.go:1080) â€” three semantically-related HTTP-protocol/version sets across 3 components with DIVERGENT membership. Suppressed: DISTINCT NAMES. Canonical-name join is lower(name) underscores-stripped, NO fuzzy/stem matching (defaultprotocols != allowedversions != defaultalpn), so the name-keyed set-diff produces no group â€” by design. This is precisely the deferred fully-semantic IF-6 case (same concept, no shared symbol name); the deterministic slice correctly stays silent (precision-first: a false 'same concept' merge is the trust-killer).

Type name 'Policy' appears in 2 components: caddyhttp/proxyprotocol/policy.go:11 (type Policy int â€” an iota enum) and caddypki/acmeserver/policy.go:12 (type Policy struct). Suppressed: ZERO-OVERLAP HOMONYM + NON-ENUMERABLE. The acmeserver Policy is a struct (not an enumerable value-set at all), the proxyprotocol Policy is an int enum; they share NO member and are different kinds entirely. IF-6 requires >=1 shared member and an enumerable set on both sides â€” neither holds. Unrelated homonyms, not a divergence.

SupportedProtocols (caddytls/values.go:120) and unsupportedProtocols (values.go:127) are named map sets, but both live in the SAME component (caddytls). Suppressed: SAME-COMPONENT â€” IF-6 requires >=2 DISTINCT components (the moat gate); two same-area sets are local linter (no-redeclare) territory, not a cross-boundary contract divergence. (Also distinct names.) Representative of all the named map[string] sets, which each appear in exactly one component.

per_family.go_specific_note: Exhaustive sweep of 318 Go files across the 6 components (caddyconfig/ cmd/ internal/ modules/ notify/ + root). ZERO genuine IF-6 fires. Three Go-specific structural facts: (1) Carrier forms in Go = `var NAME = []string{...}` (7 module-level named sets found, e.g. defaultProtocols, defaultALPN, safeHeaders, hopHeaders, defaultMIMETypes, defaultIndexNames, allowedVersions, defaultDirectiveOrder), typed enums (`type T string` + a const block of members â€” e.g. ACMEChallenge=http-01/dns-01/tls-alpn-01 in acmeserver, defined once), iota int enums (only ONE in the whole tree: Policy in proxyprotocol), and named map allowlists (`var X = map[string]...` â€” SupportedProtocols, SupportedCurves, networkTypes, etc.). (2) A name-collision scan (canonicalized lower(name), underscores stripped) over EVERY []string var, typed-enum type name, typed string-const enum, and iota enum found exactly ONE cross-component same-name pair â€” MatcherNameCtxKey â€” and it is a same-value scalar const (suppressed on same-value + non-enumerable). Every actual value-SET is defined in exactly one component, so the name-keyed set-diff has no >=2-component group to compare anywhere in the repo. (3) Go's typed-const-enum idiom (one type per package, members in a const block) plus single-package ownership of each module means the divergent-named-set shape is structurally rare here â€” the related-but-differently-named protocol/version sets (defaultProtocols / allowedVersions / defaultALPN) are exactly the deferred fully-semantic IF-6 case the deterministic slice intentionally does not reach. Precision outcome: 0 fires / 0 false alarms; a naive same-name-or-similar-concept matcher would have over-fired on the 3 protocol sets and on MatcherNameCtxKey â€” the guarded predicate correctly names each as predicate-FALSE.

suppressed.reason: NOT-MODULE-LEVEL + MUTABLE-BINDING: `skipInstallTrust := false` is a function-local `:=` variable, not a module-level `const`, and it is rebound to `true` at line 204 (`skipInstallTrust = true`). The shipped predicate's `const`/no-rebind requirement (Go `const` forbids rebind) is FALSE on both counts, so the `if skipInstallTrust`-style guard never anchors. A naive `if <bool-ident> {` scanner would chase this; the guarded detector stays silent.

MUTABLE-BINDING / non-const local: `falseBool := false` is a local `:=` binding used as an addressable `*bool` sink (not a module-level literal const), so it is not a compile-time constant gate; predicate-FALSE by the mutable-binding clause.

NON-BOOL const block: `const ( ExitCodeSuccess = iota; ... )` is an integer iota enumeration, not a `= true|false` literal. The IF-10 slice fires only on a bool-literal const used as the WHOLE test of an `if`; an int const never folds a boolean branch. Predicate-FALSE: not a compile-time literal bool.

NON-BOOL typed-string const block (`replaceAction filterAction = "replace"`, etc.) and the line 662 numeric block (`maxRegexpOperations = 50`). These are string/int consts, never bool literals â€” no boolean branch can be statically folded by them. Predicate-FALSE: RHS is not a true/false literal.
NON-BOOL const block: `DefaultHTTPPort = 80`, `DefaultHTTPSPort = 443` are int consts. No bool-literal const exists to gate an `if CONST {`; predicate-FALSE (not a compile-time literal bool).

MUTABLE-BINDING (the `var (...)` block analog): `space = true`, `beginningOfLine = true` are function-local `var`-block initializers (the surrounding declaration is `var`, not `const`) that are reassigned throughout the loop (e.g. `space = false` at lines 122/170). The value changes at runtime â†’ suppress by the mutable-binding clause; not a module-level const.

per_family.go_specific_note: caddy@fcba554d (318 Go files / ~71.5k LOC) contains ZERO module-level boolean-literal consts. Exhaustive sweeps confirmed it: (1) no `const NAME = true|false` (single-line, any whitespace), (2) no `const NAME bool = ...`, (3) no `= true|false` inside any `const (...)` block â€” all 32 const blocks hold only `iota` enums, ints, durations, or typed-string consts, and (4) every `= true|false` hit in the repo is a function-local `var`/`:=`/struct-field assignment (mutable), never a package-level const. Because the shipped IF-10 slice anchors strictly on a module-level literal-CONSTANT bool used as the WHOLE test of a conditional, and that declaration form is absent, the detector has no anchor and correctly produces 0 fires. Go-specific structural facts worth recording: (a) Go's `const` keyword does forbid rebind (a true compile-time constant, so the Â§25/Â§29 const-fold semantics map cleanly onto Go), and Go consts are typed â€” so a Go bool const WOULD be a valid IF-10 anchor if one existed; it just doesn't here. (b) Idiomatic Go expresses build/debug toggles via `var` (mutable) or, for true compile-time dead code, via build tags / `//go:build` constraints and the `const enabled = false` build-tag pattern in separate files â€” none of which appear as an in-source `const NAME=false; if NAME {` form in this repo. (c) Go's compiler itself reports `if false {` dead branches only weakly and never const-folds a named bool const into an unreachable-code diagnostic, which is exactly the linter-silence gap IF-10 targets â€” but the gap is moot here for lack of any such const. Net: family is structurally applicable to the Go stack but yields no fires on this mature codebase; the deliverable is the named-suppression precision discipline (mutable locals and non-bool const blocks correctly NOT fired).

suppressed.reason: Deliberate documented ignore, NOT a bare empty swallow: `_, _ = io.Copy(buf, req.Body) // can't handle error, so just ignore it`. The error is explicitly discarded with a rationale comment on the happy-path body-buffering of a request placeholder (and again at line 251). IF-7c suppress clause: a deliberate ignore / non-empty handling (explicit `_, _ =` discard satisfying errcheck) is not a silent cross-boundary swallow.
Same deliberate documented io.Copy discard as line 236 (`// can't handle error, so just ignore it`). Suppressed: documented intentional ignore, not an empty handler.

Deliberate documented graceful-degrade: `// We intentionally ignore the error since it's not fatal if the PKI app is not explicitly configured`; the err==nil path sets the field, the error path just skips. Also the enclosing `Provision` is a method on the UNEXPORTED type `adminAPI`, so not a cross-component-consumed exported symbol. Suppressed on both gates (deliberate ignore + private receiver).

Deliberate documented ignore: `// ignore error here because we can't handle it anyway` on `matches, _ := path.Match(...)`. Enclosing function returns bool (a match predicate), not error; the discard is explicit and rationale'd. Suppressed (deliberate ignore; non-error-returning function).

Linter-acknowledged deliberate ignore: `//nolint:errcheck` on `http2.ConfigureServer(srv.server, h2server)`. An explicit suppression annotation = a documented intentional drop, the polar opposite of a silent swallow. Suppressed.
Second `//nolint:errcheck` deliberate ignore in app.go; same rationale as line 512. Suppressed (documented intentional ignore).

`if err != nil { continue }` in an origin-collection loop (url.Parse) â€” a NON-EMPTY handler with deliberate skip-and-continue control flow (a malformed origin is excluded from the allowed set). IF-7c keys strictly on an empty/comment-only body; a `continue` is deliberate log-free skip, not a bare swallow. Suppressed (non-empty deliberate-skip handler).

`if err != nil { return false }` inside `(*Server).hasListenerAddress` â€” an UNEXPORTED method returning bool (a predicate). `return false` on a parse error correctly means 'no matching listener'; the function returns no error to swallow. Suppressed: not error-returning, not exported (not cross-consumed).

`if err != nil { continue }` in the listener-address loop of the same unexported predicate method `hasListenerAddress`. Deliberate skip of an unparseable address; non-empty handler; private function. Suppressed.

`if err != nil { return false }` inside the UNEXPORTED function `listenersUseAnyPortOtherThan` returning bool. `return false` on a non-numeric port is the correct predicate result; no error is returned/swallowed. Suppressed: not error-returning, not exported.
`if err != nil { continue }` in the same unexported bool-predicate function; deliberate skip of an unparseable address. Suppressed (non-empty handler, private function).
`if err != nil { continue }` in a candidate-file loop â€” deliberate skip-and-try-next control flow, not a bare empty swallow. Non-empty handler. Suppressed (deliberate skip).

`if err != nil { return false }` inside `(*FileServer).isSymlinkTargetDir` â€” an UNEXPORTED method returning bool. `return false` on an fs.Stat error correctly means 'target is not a known directory'; no error return is swallowed. Suppressed: not error-returning, not exported.

`if err != nil { return nil }` (and lines 239, 244) inside the `fetchConfig` closure of a TEST-HELPER package (`caddytest/`, not one of the 6 product components). The closure returns `any` for a poll/retry loop (caller retries 10x), so `return nil` is part of a deliberate retry design, not an error swallow. Suppressed: test infrastructure + local closure + non-error return type.

`if err != nil { return 0 }` is a FUZZ-HARNESS skip (return 0 = 'uninteresting input') â€” by design the fuzzer feeds malformed input. Test/fuzz infrastructure, not a cross-consumed product error path. Suppressed (also: listeners_fuzz.go:21, caddyconfig/caddyfile/lexer_fuzz.go:21, modules/caddyhttp/templates/frontmatter_fuzz.go:21 â€” identical fuzz-skip shape).

per_family.go_specific_note: Family is structurally applicable (Go has explicit error values + a clear exported/unexported boundary and component dirs), but produced ZERO fires across 318 Go files / 216 non-test files. Key Go-specific structural facts that anchor the precision-first silence: (1) **`errcheck` is enabled in CI** (.golangci.yml line 27, `tests: false`), so EVERY silently-dropped error return is linter-forced into either an explicit `_ =` discard, an `exclude-functions` entry (fmt.*, zap encoders), or a `//nolint:errcheck` annotation â€” i.e. every surviving discard is a DELIBERATE, documented ignore, which is exactly the IF-7c suppress clause. (2) The canonical Go swallow `if err != nil {}` (empty body) appears NOWHERE in the repo (0 matches, single- or multi-line). (3) The Go bool-predicate idiom produces look-alike `if err != nil { return false }` blocks that a naive detector would over-fire on; the guarded predicate correctly stays silent because the enclosing function returns bool (a predicate result), NOT error â€” there is no error value being swallowed (hasListenerAddress, listenersUseAnyPortOtherThan, isSymlinkTargetDir, all also unexported). (4) Loop `if err != nil { continue }` is deliberate skip-and-proceed control flow (non-empty handler), not a bare swallow. (5) All `_ = ...err` / `_, _ = io.Copy(...)` discards in product code carry an explicit rationale comment ("can't handle the error", "intentionally ignore", "non-fatal"). (6) Of the function-scoped exported-AND-cross-component-consumed gate: the only structural near-candidates were either unexported (lowercase receiver/func), bool-returning predicates, or in the caddytest/ test-helper and *_fuzz.go harnesses â€” none is a bare empty error handler inside a cross-component-consumed exported function. Result: a mature, error-propagating codebase with zero real IF-7c fires and ~15 named suppressions, consistent with the prompt's expectation.

fires.claim: The cmd component reaches across the boundary to directly re-assign the root caddy package's exported mutable globals caddy.ConfigAutosavePath and caddy.DefaultStorage (cmd/main.go:376-377), duplicating the origin derivation in storage.go:157,160 verbatim with no setter/interface; read back by cmd (storagefuncs.go:95,167; commandfuncs.go:228) and root caddy internally -> silent shared-state coupling: if storage.go changes how the default is derived, cmd's re-inlined copy silently diverges.

suppressed.reason: EXPLICIT REGISTRY + DOCUMENTED INTERFACE. The global module registry `modules`/`modulesMu` is unexported (Go: physically unreachable from another package) and mutated only via the documented exported funcs RegisterModule (line 138), GetModule (164), GetModules (204), under an RWMutex. Every cross-component access goes through the documented registration interface -> not silent coupling. Predicate clause 'no explicit interface' is FALSE.

INTRA-COMPONENT + DOCUMENTED + GUARDED. currentCtx/rawCfg/rawCfgJSON/rawCfgIndex are unexported root-caddy globals, doc-commented, guarded by currentCtxMu/rawCfgMu, and read/written only within the root caddy component (caddy.go + admin.go are the same package/component) via the public Run/Load/admin API. No cross-component boundary is crossed -> 'reached across a component boundary' is FALSE.

INTRA-COMPONENT + DOCUMENTED REGISTRATION. directiveOrder (mutable slice) is mutated only within the caddyconfig/httpcaddyfile package (directives.go, options.go, httptype.go all one component) via the exported RegisterDirective/RegisterDirectiveOrder funcs and the parse-time 'order' global-option handler. Single-package scope, documented interface -> the cross-component-boundary clause is FALSE.

DOCUMENTED SINGLETON + EXPLICIT INTERFACE. DefaultFileSystem is a documented default singleton; the FileSystemMap uses a sync.Map with documented Register/Unregister/Get/Default methods. Access is mediated by an explicit documented interface, not direct shared-var mutation -> suppress clause 'documented singletons / explicit registries' applies.

EXPLICIT REGISTRY (unexported). networkTypes is an unexported map mutated only via the documented exported RegisterNetwork (line 664) under a mutex; other components register networks through that interface, never by touching the var (Go forbids it). 'no explicit interface' is FALSE.

EXPLICIT REGISTRY (unexported). configAdapters is unexported, mutated only via the exported RegisterAdapter (line 111) and read via GetAdapter (121). Documented registration interface -> 'no explicit interface' is FALSE.

INTRA-COMPONENT. adminMetrics/globalMetrics are unexported root-caddy globals initialized in metrics.go init() and read in admin.go/context.go/caddy.go -- all the SAME root caddy component. No component boundary is crossed; the prometheus collectors are also internally synchronized -> not cross-component coupling.

DOCUMENTED REF-COUNTED POOL (UsagePool), intra-component. `writers` (and `listenerPool` in listeners.go:736) are unexported caddy.UsagePool instances accessed via the documented LoadOrNew/Delete/References API within the root caddy component; other components never touch the var. Explicit interface present -> suppress.

TEST-ONLY + THIRD-PARTY VAR. smallstepacme.InsecurePortHTTP01 is mutated only inside a caddytest integration test, setting/resetting a vendored third-party package's port for the test fixture. Test scope, not a production component coupling, and the var belongs to an external dependency -> out of scope for a production silent-coupling fire.

per_family.go_specific_note: Decisive Go-specific structural fact that collapses IF-3a's surface here: a package-level var with a lowercase (unexported) name is PHYSICALLY unreachable from any other Go package, so it cannot be 'reached across a component boundary' at all. Consequently the only way one Caddy component can silently share-mutate another's package-level state is an EXPORTED (capitalized) var assigned directly across the package boundary with no setter. Caddy's pervasive idiom -- unexported global map/slice/pool + an exported RegisterX/GetX function (RegisterModule, RegisterNetwork, RegisterAdapter, RegisterDirective, FileSystemMap.Register, UsagePool) -- means each registry AUTOMATICALLY satisfies the predicate's 'explicit documented interface' escape clause and is correctly suppressed. The compiler-enforced export boundary plus mutex/sync.Map guarding makes this a mature, low-coupling codebase: out of ~30 package-level vars surveyed across the 6 components, exactly TWO exported mutable globals (caddy.DefaultStorage, caddy.ConfigAutosavePath) are mutated cross-component (by cmd/main.go) without an interface -> the lone LOW fire (a benign single-threaded startup re-derivation whose only real hazard is the duplicated derivation expression silently diverging from storage.go's origin). Also note: the metamorphic IF-3a substrate in Go is rarer than in dynamic-language web stacks because there is no shared global ORM/DB-session singleton -- Caddy threads config via the Context and StorageConverter interface rather than a mutable global DB handle, eliminating the classic IF-3a 'shared table/session written by two components' shape entirely.

naive_vs_guarded_illustrative.note: A naive surface detector would over-fire on ~35 near-candidates: IF-8 ~4 (the size-5 component-bucket SCC {caddyconfig,cmd,internal,modules,root} with 4 mutual pairs, all artifacts of collapsing a clean package DAG onto 6 oversized buckets); IF-6 ~4 (the 3 divergent-but-distinctly-named HTTP protocol/version sets defaultProtocols/allowedVersions/defaultALPN + the same-value MatcherNameCtxKey scalar); IF-10 ~3 (mutable function-local bool bindings skipInstallTrust/falseBool/space chased by an `if <bool-ident>` scanner); IF-7c ~15 (bool-predicate `if err!=nil{return false}` look-alikes in unexported hasListenerAddress/listenersUseAnyPortOtherThan/isSymlinkTargetDir, loop `continue` skips, fuzz harnesses, and rationale-commented/`//nolint:errcheck` discards); IF-3a ~9 (registry/intra-component/test package-level vars). The guarded predicate fired ONCE (the real LOW exported-global coupling) with 0 false defects â€” every suppression names its predicate-FALSE mechanism (disjoint-package bucket artifact vs real SCC=0; same-value/distinct-name/non-enumerable; mutable-binding/non-bool-const; deliberate-documented-ignore/bool-predicate/unexported/test-harness; explicit-registry/intra-component).

key_go_findings: IF-8 / Go compile-time cycle ban: a buildable Go module CANNOT contain a true package import cycle (the compiler rejects it). Verified by building the real 46-package / 195-edge import graph and running Tarjan SCC â†’ 0 strongly-connected components of sizeâ‰Ą2 (proven DAG). The coarse 6-bucket component graph DOES yield a spurious size-5 SCC, which a naive component-level IF-8 detector would over-fire on â€” the guarded predicate correctly suppresses it (every mutual bucket pair is DISJOINT packages on each side, a pure granularity artifact, not a real cyclic dependency).

IF-3a surface collapse: Go's unexported-is-physically-unreachable rule + Caddy's pervasive `unexported var + exported RegisterX/GetX` registry idiom collapse the cross-component shared-state surface to exactly TWO exported mutable globals â€” the lone fire (cmd/main.go re-assigns caddy.DefaultStorage / caddy.ConfigAutosavePath cross-component with no setter; LOW, a minor coupling/code-smell, recorded NOT filed).

IF-7c suppression by CI: caddy's CI-enforced `errcheck` forces every surviving error discard into a documented `_ =` / `//nolint:errcheck` ignore that satisfies the IF-7c deliberate-ignore suppress clause; the bool-predicate `if err!=nil{return false}` idiom is recognized as no-error-to-swallow, not a swallow (15 named suppressions, 0 fires).
honest_assessment: This is the FIRST Go-stack datapoint in the discovery track, and it exercises five cross-module families (IF-8 import-cycle, IF-6 divergent named set, IF-10 const-gated dead branch, IF-7c swallowed error, IF-3a silent shared-state coupling) against a mature, error-propagating codebase â€” landing on the expected near-silent outcome of 1 LOW fire / 38 named suppressions / 0 false defects. The decisive Go-specific structural findings are real comprehension, not keyword-matching: the compile-time package import-cycle ban means a buildable module CANNOT contain a true cycle (verified by building the 46-package / 195-edge import graph and running Tarjan SCC â†’ 0 cycles), so IF-8 correctly refuses to fire on the lossy component-bucket SCC; the unexported-name-is-physically-unreachable rule plus Caddy's pervasive `unexported var + exported RegisterX/GetX` registry idiom collapses IF-3a's surface to exactly two exported cross-component globals (the lone fire); CI-enforced `errcheck` forces every surviving discard into a documented `_ =` / `//nolint` ignore that satisfies IF-7c's suppress clause; and the bool-predicate `if err!=nil{return false}` idiom is recognized as no-error-to-swallow rather than a swallow. The suite stayed correctly silent where the shapes were predicate-FALSE â€” distinguishing real cyclic dependency from grouping artifact, divergent-membership sets from same-value/distinct-name/scalar cases, and compile-time const folds from mutable locals â€” demonstrating comprehension of Go semantics over surface pattern-matching.
counts_note: Authoritative counts are the structured suppressed[] arrays: 38 named suppressions (IF-8 4 / IF-6 4 / IF-10 6 / IF-7c 15 / IF-3a 9) + 1 LOW IF-3a fire, 0 false defects. (The synthesis agent's free-text initially said 41 by a manual miscount; reconciled to the data.)
